title: Budget constraint
body:
In economics, a budget constraint represents all the combinations of goods and services that a consumer (or other decision-maker) can purchase given current prices and a given level of income or wealth. In consumer theory, the budget constraint and a preference map (or system of indifference curves) are the basic tools used to analyse consumer choice. In the standard two-good case, the budget constraint can be represented graphically as a straight line showing the trade-off between the two goods. If 
  
    
      
        x
      
    
    {\displaystyle x}
  
 and 
  
    
      
        y
      
    
    {\displaystyle y}
  
 denote the quantities of two goods, with prices 
  
    
      
        
          P
          
            x
          
        
      
    
    {\displaystyle P_{x}}
  
 and 
  
    
      
        
          P
          
            y
          
        
      
    
    {\displaystyle P_{y}}
  
, and 
  
    
      
        m
      
    
    {\displaystyle m}
  
 denotes income, the budget line is given by

  
    
      
        
          P
          
            x
          
        
        x
        +
        
          P
          
            y
          
        
        y
        =
        m
        .
      
    
    {\displaystyle P_{x}x+P_{y}y=m.}
  

Solving for 
  
    
      
        y
      
    
    {\displaystyle y}
  
 yields

  
    
      
        y
        =

        −

        ,
      
    
    {\displaystyle y={\frac {m}{P_{y}}}-{\frac {P_{x}}{P_{y}}}\,x,}
  

where

          P
          
            y
          
        
      
    
    {\displaystyle m/P_{y}}
  
 is the vertical intercept (the maximum amount of 
  
    
      
        y
      
    
    {\displaystyle y}
  
 the consumer can buy if

        =
        0
      
    
    {\displaystyle x=0}
  
) and 
  
    
      
        −
        
          P

          /

    {\displaystyle -P_{x}/P_{y}}
  
 is the slope of the budget line, representing the opportunity cost of one more unit of 
  
    
      
        x
      
    
    {\displaystyle x}
  
 in terms of 
  
    
      
        y
      
    
    {\displaystyle y}
  
 forgone. Similar constraints appear in models of labour–leisure choice, intertemporal consumption, firm behaviour and international trade.
